summary: Tencent Holdings (SEHK:700) is reported to be facing halted deliveries of advanced Nvidia AI chips after intervention from Chinese authorities, despite prior approval from the U.S. government. headline: As chip industry chases AI, U.S. national labs look to newcomers for supercomputers
summary: In a nondescript building on Kirtland Air Force Base on the high desert of New Mexico, liquid-cooled supercomputers gurgle and hum their way through some of the most complex math problems the U.S. government seeks to solve: simulating how hypersonic nuclear weapons would ‌move through the earth's atmosphere, or what would happen if one nuclear warhead detonated near another.  For more than a decade, the chips handling this secretive and demanding work ‌came from mainstream semiconductor firms like Nvidia or Advanced Micro Devices.  But with those companies increasingly designing their chips for artificial intelligence and facing supply shortages, the managers in charge of the systems at Sandia National Laboratories, which operates ​the machines at Kirtland and is one of three U.S. labs tasked with developing and maintaining the nation's nuclear weapons arsenal, are increasingly unsure how they will find computing power for high-precision scientific work like theirs.

content.title: As chip industry chases AI, U.S. national labs look to newcomers for supercomputers
content.summary: In a nondescript building on Kirtland Air Force Base on the high desert of New Mexico, liquid-cooled supercomputers gurgle and hum their way through some of the most complex math problems the U.S. government seeks to solve: simulating how hypersonic nuclear weapons would ‌move through the earth's atmosphere, or what would happen if one nuclear warhead detonated near another.  For more than a decade, the chips handling this secretive and demanding work ‌came from mainstream semiconductor firms like Nvidia or Advanced Micro Devices.  But with those companies increasingly designing their chips for artificial intelligence and facing supply shortages, the managers in charge of the systems at Sandia National Laboratories, which operates ​the machines at Kirtland and is one of three U.S. labs tasked with developing and maintaining the nation's nuclear weapons arsenal, are increasingly unsure how they will find computing power for high-precision scientific work like theirs.

thumbnail.caption: A chip from NextSilicon lies at the Computer Science Research Institute at Sandia National Laboratories
